data.title: FTEC: The Right Core, But Not The Complete Picture
data.description:
The Fidelity MSCI Information Technology Index ETF (NYSEARCA:FTEC) tracks the MSCI USA IMI Information Technology 25/50 Index, much like VGT, and suffers from the same drawbacks. My first preference in today's market environment is a DIY portfolio, even in tech. More so because the valuations are stretched and not all ETFs are not ideally loaded, dragged by a backward looking market cap factor.

However, that standard advice aside, FTEC additionally suffers from lower AUM related issues, like bid ask spreads, compared to even VGT, which makes it even more difficult to Buy FTEC. The marginally lower fees at 0.08% (vs 0.09% for VGT) is minor nitpicking in the absence of any strong case for FTEC. Hence, FTEC is assigned a similar rating as VGT i.e. a Hold. This thesis will focus on how the setup is not favorable for tech focused portfolios like FTEC and VUG both, from some additional data and fundamental analysis I did.

Drawback Recap

The use of GICS classification means names like Amazon, Meta and Alphabet remain outside the portfolio. It is not a question of whether FTEC's current holdings are good enough. It is a question of inclusion for a thematic ETF in technology.

The other issue with the methodology is the backward looking market cap weighting that results in disproportionate concentration in the top 10. These levels of concentration is one of the highest in history. While concentration is still acceptable for a tech portfolio, FTEC takes it to another extreme. For a portfolio of close to 300 stocks, a top 3 contributing ~45%, reduces the presence of the last 100 names to almost nothing more than window dressing. And that is exactly why I am advocating DIY investing in tech today. The core portfolio diversification benefits in passive investing is looking lost, and instead investors are left with a backward looking rigid holding that reacts slowly.

Odds Against GICS Tech Portfolio

A check on total return of a GICS constrained portfolio against the a simple equal weighted magnificent 7 ETF like MAGS shows significant outperformance in the latter approach.

And the outperformance does not imply a deeper drawdown in stress scenarios either. In the recent tariff sell-off, MAGS was down as far as FTEC was, almost. I expect that to be the norm in theory given the diversification benefits of a highly disparate constituent behavior across the volatile tech stock collection. However, the outperformance is a surprise, although that is not a structural advantage. The gains in Meta have led that upside in the period of analysis. In another era, FTEC could gain if its bets on Nvidia and Microsoft outperforms.

To check performance over a larger tenure, I picked FNGS with a very highly concentrated bet on the broader tech mega caps including Amazon, Meta and Google. For a slightly worse drawdown profile (checked against the 2022 corrections), the returns differences are again impressively better.

Intra Tech Views

Lets pitch Nvidia, Apple and Microsoft against Amazon, Meta and Google and see how the former 3 (FTEC trio) are placed against the missing names (missing trio). The past performance is something that we have already seen where the latter 3's inclusion has driven up returns. Lets look at expectations ahead and factors that could predict future performance/risks.

The FTEC trio looks visibly the more quality, moat and capital discipline play in comparison to the missing trio. In my view, Microsoft remains the most convincing long-term compounder with end to end AI monetization potential and strong network effects. Apple appears growth constrained with the story more around capital return and income at this point. Nvidia tips the growth angle for the FTEC trio - comfortably the best growth story on paper across even the missing trio. However, it does carry elevated risks given valuations priced for perfection and ecosystem uncertainties.

On the other side, the missing trio seem to offer better growth overall (continuing the trend from the past). Risks are looking slightly higher than the FTEC trio, but not as significantly different as the upside is. Amazon is currently looking struck, but offers a long-term growth optionality through a multitude of diversified avenues. Retail margins are turning around a bit, ad monetization is kicking in and the AI infra play is promising. Meta seems to be the biggest optionality play - if execution matches the vision and potential then the upside is large here. But risks are elevated from spending and monetization unknowns. Google looks undervalued on Cloud and Waymo optionality, but also faces structural risks to margins and regulatory heat .

The Hold on FTEC

I feel the FTEC trio will be the core of my own DIY portfolio because its combines decent defense alongside growth levers from Nvidia. However, Amazon, Google and Meta will also earn their place for the optionality. The tactical rotation around the FTEC core could result in strong outperformance as seen historically. Unfortunately, FTEC does not have the luxury of tactical or even long-term diversification benefits.

FTEC is not a failing proposition. As mentioned, it gets the core right, even looking ahead from a tech portfolio perspective. But I am more comfortable with diversified tech if I have to do passive investing. Even QQQ is better placed for me now, given its more defensive allocation and lower concentration risks (although still quite high on an absolute basis).

FTEC is a Hold. And not only because tech valuations are looking discomforting. Or its holdings are not aligned to my viewpoint. I am more concerned overall about new age tech companies missing the bus here. That and the fact that if I have to go passive, I will like the comfort of more diversification. A seen in the past, a diversified tech approach works better in terms of overall risk reward profiles.

data.title: Credit Rating For The Unrated (Part 12): CTO Realty Growth
data.description:
We continue our series with CTO Realty Growth, Inc. (NYSE:CTO) and its outstanding preferred stock, CTO Realty Growth, Inc. 6.375% Series A Cumulative Redeemable Preferred Stock (NYSE:CTO.PR.A).

Company Overview

CTO Realty Growth, Inc. is a publicly traded real estate investment trust that owns and operates a portfolio of high-quality, retail-based properties located primarily in higher growth markets in the United States. CTO also externally manages and owns a meaningful interest in Alpine Income Property Trust, Inc. (PINE), a publicly traded net lease REIT.

The company has $1.24 billion in total assets, and as of the writing of this article, the market cap is $570 million. CTO total return for the past 10 years is 38%, underperforming both VNQ and SPY.

CTO pays $0.38 dividend each quarter, which at current prices is about 8.78%.

The forward P/FFO is 9.38, and the forward funds from operations yield comes at 5.3%. Key valuation metrics are displayed below:

The company is focused on open-air shopping centres in the Southwest and Southeast. Please see below a slide from their Q1 2025 presentation:

The portfolio composition is also described in the presentation:

  • Asset coverage ratio of 206%
  • Earns 9.45% gross in rental
  • Operating expenses, excluding depreciation, are approximately 38.79% of the total revenue
  • Interest expenses are 3.82% of the debt
  • Asset yield is 10.61% and market adjusted is 10.98%

Preferred Stock

There is only one preferred - CTO Realty Growth, Inc. 6.375% Series A Cumulative Redeemable Preferred Stock. Below we have listed its key metrics.

The security has a current yield of 7.49% that is fixed and cumulative. It also has a change of control clause, which is the lesser of Par Value (25) + accrued dividends or a conversion of 2.8218 per share. It must be noted that the conversion clause in the prospectus is 0.9406 per share, but since there was a stock split 3:1 in 2022, it adjusts to 2.8218 per share.

Credit Rating

We follow Moody's methodology with some adjustments to evaluate the creditworthiness of the company.

1. Gross assets: The total assets of 1.3 billion fall into Ba3 category

2. Unencumbered assets: Percentage of 49% is defined as Ba2

3. Secured debt: With 32% of secured debt to gross assets we assign a B2 rating

4. Total debt + preferred stock : 54% is leading us to Ba2 rating

5. Net debt to EBITDA: Using total debt and EBITDA of $79 million we are coming to a rating of Caa2

6. Fixed charge coverage. EBITDA/(interest and dividend payment on its preferred stock), the company scores Ba3

The balance sheet score and the adjusted overall score are both Ba3. In the adjusted balance sheet score, we have taken into consideration:

  • Market Positioning - B1
  • Operating Environment - B1
  • Access to Capital - Baa3

CTO has focused its open-air retail portfolio in places with fast growth, positive job migration, and concentrated population:

As usual, we score the preferred stock two notches lower to B2, which for current yield of 7.49%, when you have A1 preferreds trading at 6.50% is a bit overpriced.

Before we conclude, we must address the elephant in the room, which is the short report from Wolfpack. We do not own CTO or CTO.PR.A, as neither of them fit our criteria for yield to risk ratio. This being said, if the allegations are true, they would have an impact on our credit score. However, we are using official data regulated by the SEC, and as investors, we rely on the regulatory body to do its job in such cases. We hope that management will address any doubts at the upcoming earnings on the 29th of July 2025, and we will write an update if needed. Our recommendation will be for the preferred stock investors to switch to a different instrument, not because of the short report, but the 7.49% current yield for a B2 rating is not adequate in today's market environment. You can buy investment-grade instruments with the same yield.

Conclusion

CTO Realty Growth scores Ba3 rating and the preferred B2. We find the yield of the preferred underwhelming at current prices and recommend switching to a different security.

data.title: Alphabet: Who Said Search Can't Still Be Sexy?
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The last time I wrote about Alphabet Inc. aka Google (GOOG, NASDAQ:GOOGL), in April 2025, I analyzed the company’s Q1 earnings report and examined how the management was navigating the macroeconomic challenges and what impact the challenges at the time had on the company’s capex spending. I also examined the progress made by both Google Cloud and Waymo. I had a Buy rating on the stock.

Since the article was published, the stock has gained 19.5%, outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500), which gained 15.4% during the same period.

In this article, I analyze the company’s Q2 earnings report and examine the key takeaways from the quarter and their implications on investors. I also deploy a predictive model using machine learning to determine GOOGL’s valuation.

A Snapshot of Second Quarter Results

Alphabet had a good quarter in my opinion, beating on both the top- and bottom-line estimates once again. More specifically, Q2 revenues came in at $96.43 billion, up 14% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $2.45 billion. Diluted EPS came in at $2.31, up 22% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $0.12. Operating margins came in strong at 32.4% but were flat y/y on account of higher legal costs, with a $1.4 billion charge related to a settlement being the primary driver of operating expenses. Free cash flows for the quarter, on account of higher capital expenditures, dipped to $5.3 billion, with trailing twelve-month cash flows coming in at $66.7 billion.

While management didn’t provide any specific outlook, they did provide commentary on what to expect for the rest of the year and for 2026. The key takeaway from the commentary is that the company plans to raise their capex spending to $85 billion from the previous guidance of $75 billion. Management cited strong demand for the company’s cloud products and services as the primary reason behind raising capex spend.

More specifically, CFO Anat Ashkenazi cited “additional investment in servers, the timing of delivery of servers, and an acceleration in the pact of data centre construction,” as the factors behind the $10 billion jump in capex spend. Management also announced that higher depreciation, on account of the significant increase in Capex would act as a headwind to P&L. Finally, with respect to headcount, management announced that they would see a sequential increase in total headcount in the third quarter.

Search is Not Dead…At Least Not Yet

There have been a lot of claims in recent times that, with the advent of AI models such as ChatGPT and Perplexity, it would be curtains down for GOOGL’s search business. Q2 proved that these claims may have been exaggerated.

The company’s AI overviews have certainly proved to be popular and are driving higher search usage. Management announced that AI overviews has now over 2 billion monthly users across more than 200 countries, and the feature is available in 40 languages. Management also reported that AI overviews drove a 10% increase in search usage in Q2, which demonstrates that the company is leveraging AI to maintain its search dominance despite facing one of the most challenging competitive landscapes in recent memory. Moreover, the company’s newly launched AI mode already has over 100 million monthly active users despite being launched only in the U.S. and India.

All of these developments ensured that the search business not only went back to double-digit growth on a y/y basis but also comprehensively reversed its sequential decline that it witnessed last quarter.

One good quarter doesn’t make a trend, just like how one bad quarter doesn’t make one, either. However, the progress made by AI Overviews certainly suggests that the growth potential of the Search Business continues to remain strong - albeit it is not as bulletproof as it was in the past. Moreover, it is also encouraging to see how GOOGL’s AI features are ensuring that the Search segment is maintaining its popularity amongst younger users. For instance, the combination of lens or Circle to Search along with AI overviews saw strong traction among younger users.

The traditional way of searching via Google may no longer be a trend in the age of AI. However, the fact that GOOGL was able to transform its search business using its AI features certainly bodes well for the company’s future and certainly suggests that the demise of the company’s Search business may indeed have been exaggerated.

YouTube is Not Just Clocking Milestones

In my last article on GOOGL, I mentioned how in the previous quarter YouTube reached a historic milestone when it celebrated its 20th birthday. At the time, data from Nielsen had shown that the platform accounted for 12% of the total TV watch-time, which was a new record.

This quarter was no different, as the latest Nielsen data showed that consumers watch YouTube on TV more than on their phones or any other device, with an average of more than 1 billion hours each day. This viewing time is more than the total viewing time on Disney’s broadcast Network, on a dozen-plus cable channels, and on three streaming services - combined.

While milestones are great, it means nothing if they are not backed by revenue growth, as far as investors are concerned. In Q1, YouTube ads revenues barely crossed double-digit growth on a y/y basis. Moreover, they marked a double-digit decline sequentially, which was a concern. Q2, however, was different, with the segment registering a stronger growth on a y/y basis, and the sequential growth surging into positive territory.

Furthermore, management also announced that YouTube Shorts now average over 200 billion daily views and the company’s AI tools have improved not only recommendations but also offer features such as auto dubbing, which have increased engagement.

Finally, in addition to ads, the company is also focusing on growing the YouTube subscription businesses, as evidenced by the expansion of its premium light offerings to 15 new countries. Focusing on growing its subscription revenues is certainly the right step in my opinion, especially since in addition to content from independent creators, the platform also plays hosts to NFL Sunday Ticket, which costs the company $2 billion annually. Furthermore, companies like WGD and Paramount also offer consumers access to their content inside the YouTube app. As such, YouTube is well-positioned to diversify its revenue stream and should certainly capitalize on it.

Google Cloud Stands Out on Its Own, Justifying the Massive Capex Spend

Q2 saw Google Cloud generating strong revenues and margins, justifying the massive capex spend that GOOGL has been undertaking recently. More specifically, after generating a revenue growth of 28.1% y/y in Q1, the segment’s revenue growth in Q2 came in at 31.7%. Furthermore, if one looks at the performance sequentially, then Q2 was by far the best quarter ever, as the sequential growth came in at 11.1% compared to 2.6% in Q1. The last time it clocked double-digit sequential growth was in the fourth quarter of FY21, so it’s safe to say that the company’s capex spend is certainly paying off.

There was a lot of skepticism amongst analysts during the Q2 earnings call when management announced that the company would be increasing its capex spend by $10 billion to $85 billion for FY25. Management also mentioned that FY26 capex spend would also be higher, which further led analysts to question the timing of ROI. I have no issues with the company’s capex spending whatsoever. This is because not only is the spending translating into stronger growth for Google Cloud but it is also helping the company to revamp its Search business, as mentioned earlier.

There was a dip in growth in Google Cloud in Q1, which was concerning. But Q2 proved that it was merely a blip rather than a trend.

Valuation

Forward P/E Multiple Approach

Price Target

$256.00

Projected Forward P/E Multiple

22.4x

Projected EPS in the next twelve months

$12.32

Present Value of Projected EPS

$11.43

Sources: Alpha Vantage and Author’s Calculations.

I deployed an Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA) model using Machine Learning to forecast the GOOGL’s EPS in the next fourth quarters. The ARIMA model is beneficial for investors for several reasons such as more user-friendly and interpretable coefficients, automatic removal of mean-shifting trends, and the ability of the model to balance both fit and complexity through information criteria such as Akaike Information Criterion (AIC).

GOOGL’s EPS data was collected from Alpha Vantage for every fiscal quarter since 2005. In addition to EPS data, items from GOOGL’s income statement, balance sheet, and cash-flow statement were also collected. After data cleaning and merging datasets, the final sample consisted of data from 81 quarters.

The model was trained using 78 quarters with the remaining three quarters used for out-of-sample testing. The out-of-sample testing resulted in the following model metrics:

Metric

Value

Notes

Mean Absolute Error (MAE)

0.430

Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)

0.430

RMSE = MAE implies that model errors are without large outliers

Mean Absolute Percentage Error

18.61%

10-20% range implies good forecasting

Given that the metrics suggest that the model is good for forecasting, the model was then used to project the EPS for the next four quarters. This was done by re-fitting the model on the entire 81-quarter series and then projecting four steps ahead (2025 Q3 – 2026 Q2). The projected EPS from the ARIMA model are as follows:

Quarter End

Projected EPS

30-09-2025

$2.96

31-12-2025

$3.00

31-03-2026

$3.14

30-06-2026

$3.22

Based on these projections, total EPS over the next twelve months would come in at $12.32, which would represent a y/y growth of 31.2%, which is in line with the company’s long-term average.

According to Alpha Spread, GOOGL’s cost of equity is 7.82%. Using this as the discount rate results in the present value of EPS as $11.43.

According to Seeking Alpha, GOOGL current trades at a forward P/E of 19.8x, significantly lower than its 5-year average of 24.6x. The ARIMA projections have limitations such as ignoring exogenous shocks, or failing to account for the evolution of earnings processes, there is the risk of overestimating the EPS projections. After accounting for this risk, I have assumed a market multiple of 22.4x for GOOGL for my projections.

A forward P/E of 22.4x and a projected EPS of $11.43 results in a price target of $256, which represents an upside of about 35% from current levels. Despite the significant upside, investors have to factor in that GOOGL’s momentum is weak, as evidenced by the short-term momentum grade of B- and an overall grade of C+ on Seeking Alpha’s Momentum Scale. As such, while I am maintaining my Buy rating on the name, a cost-averaging strategy is recommended here.

Risk Factors

The main risk factor to my bull case remains unchanged, which is the regulatory overhang that continues to be a problem. Next month, the company is likely to hear a judgment related to the trial that challenges Google's monopoly in search and advertising. The company could also lose it’s exclusivity rights with Apple (AAPL) as a result of this trial, which could significantly impact its top- and bottom-lines.

The other risk is the potential timeline with respect to its return on its capex investments. While the company has significantly made progress with respect to its AI features, the significant increase in capex spending that is expected to continue well into 2026 does lead to the question as to when the inflection point would be achieve with respect to growth. This is yet another factor that long-term investors have to consider.

Concluding Thoughts

It was yet another strong quarter from GOOGL, with a beat on both the top- and bottom-lines. The search business returned to double-digit growth, and YouTube saw continued momentum. The biggest standout to me was unsurprisingly Google Cloud, whose revenue growth and margin expansion were strong enough to justify the company’s massive capex spending. Moreover, its AI investments are starting to pay off and against all odds, the company’s Search Business continues to remain relevant even in the age of AI.

From a valuation perspective, my ARIMA model projects strong EPS growth in the next twelve months, which makes the company very attractive at current levels. However, given the relative weak momentum, while I am maintaining my Buy rating on the stock, I do recommend a cost-averaging strategy to get in the name.

Q2 was an impressive quarter for GOOGL. But if investors must take home one key message from the quarter, it is that Search continues to be sexy.

data.title: Fox: Mastering Secular Headwinds Via Capital Discipline
data.description:
The Thesis

Fox Corporation (NASDAQ:FOX) (NASDAQ:FOXA) is an old hand at traditional media, but despite the secular headwinds the industry incurs, it still exhibits the characteristics of a resilient cash cow:

  1. moats based on hard-to-replace premium brands like Fox News and live sports rights;
  2. strong cash generation;
  3. return on invested capital consistently above the cost of capital;
  4. disciplined management that does not dilute value with expensive bets on loss-making streaming; and
  5. trading at modest multiples, despite the strong momentum over the past twelve months, with an attractive earnings yield, returning much of the profit via buybacks and dividends.

Thus, I consider FOX, in practice, as a defensive value bet where, although it doesn't grow very fast, it tends to pay well to wait.

Why Fox Continues to Create Shareholder Value

One of the reasons I like Fox as a value pick for the long haul is given it matches the two most important boxes: it generates value for its shareholders (it returns invested capital above the average cost of capital), and it also trades at relatively cheap multiples.

Emphasizing the first point, Fox Corp. makes money today mostly on ads and affiliate fees from cable operators. As an asset-light content/licensing business, it is capital efficient even though it has suffered secular headwinds pushed by cord-cutting and streaming competition. So the reputation of being a business deemed as mature, generating steady cash flows, and very strong in brand, has been put in check and should suffer equally or more than peers in the traditional TV sector.

It turns out that such secular headwinds in the industry are still no match for live TV. Fox News (cable's audience leader) and Fox Sports (NFL and other sports contracts) are the company's "premium" assets, and are harder to replace and more valuable to advertisers. And since Fox sold the movie studios, TV, and content parts of the former 21st Century Fox to Disney (DIS) in 2019, keeping only the essentials, the company without a movie studio, without betting heavily on loss-making streaming, only live content, has managed to generate a lot of cash flow, still well sustained by monetization via cable.

For example, in the last five years, levered free cash flow has been growing at a CAGR of ~8%. In a sense, this matches the idea of a mature cash cow: good cash generation, but not an ultra-growth story.

And given an industry facing secular headwinds, arguably this is a good sign, at least. Behind this cash flow resilience is a Fox that manages to return a robust profit to its shareholders. The capital employed by the company is basically in local broadcasting licenses and sports rights, so Fox doesn't need to reinvest heavily in tangible assets or loss-making streaming.

This resilient cash generation profile translates into a solid ROIC (calculated as NOPAT divided by net working capital + goodwill + other intangibles + long-term investments + deferred tax + other long-term assets), which has been around 10% for the last few years, as you can see in the chart below.

In my view, this is an indication that even without growing aggressively, Fox Corp. manages to monetize every dollar invested relatively well-something essential in an industry facing secular pressures. Although an average ROIC of ~10% (11.3% in the last twelve months) is not spectacular in itself, considering that a risk-free rate of ~4.5 in the U.S., market risk premium, and an extra premium for the traditional media sector, a WACC of ~8% I see as reasonable.

In this case, the ROIC above the WACC for Fox Corp. shows that the company still creates net economic value, with a spread of ~2 p.p. which is not huge, but I would say acceptable for a mature cash cow mainly as long as (1) it doesn't dilute this with bad reinvestments (like unprofitable streaming); and (2) it returns excess cash via dividends and buybacks—which is what Fox does. In the last twelve months alone, $1.2 billion was distributed between buybacks ($1 billion) and dividends ($270 million), that is, a distribution of 5.3% of the current market cap, which I would say is a decent yield, especially for those who see FOX as a bond proxy.

Why FOX’s Valuation Supports the Case

If the first criterion for choosing good companies to own for the long haul is based on excellent fundamentals (moats) that tend to deliver consistent returns above their cost of capital and above their opportunity cost, the second criterion for generating alpha is to determine whether that company is cheaply valued.

To do this, let's first look at FOX valuations through the lens of the operating return the business generates in relation to the total value the market pays for the entire business (equity + net debt), dividing its operating profit over the last twelve months by its enterprise value. Given the $3.02 billion in EBIT generated in that period versus the $27.7 billion in enterprise value, we arrive at an earnings yield (EBIT/EV) of precisely 10.9%.

If EBIT is stable and converted into FCF, this is relatively attractive, especially compared to a WACC of ~8%. The chart below doesn't necessarily show EBIT and FCF generation as super stable, even though I don't see any reason for extreme concern since current values for both EBIT and FCF are above their respective averages shown in the chart. Also, it is expected that in a dynamic industry, volatility should be expected given advertising markets or major sporting events.

At the moment, the earnings yield of ~11% is well above the WACC, and the ROIC of ~10% indicates that the capital employed still generates value. As a large part of the operating profit is converted into free cash flow and returned to the shareholder, the secular risks and recent volatility of EBIT tend to be offset by the capital discipline and the already discounted valuation. Thus, FOX behaves like a defensive cash cow, where the investor is remunerated even without a growth story.

What to Expect From Fox Corp’s FQ4 "Hangover" Earnings?

Fox Corp. is preparing to report its quarterly results on August 5 before the opening bell. To beat estimates, it will need to post EPS numbers above $1.00—which would be annual growth of ~11%—and revenues above $3.12 billion, an annual growth of a little less than ~1%.

While revenue revisions have been maintained by the market over the last six months, EPS revisions have been trimmed by 6.4% over the last three months. Remember that the company will face tough quarterly comps, considering that the massive revenue beat in FQ3 (by $200 million) was due to the Super Bowl bringing in $800 million + in gross ad revenue, in addition to other highlights such as Tubi growing organically by 35% YoY and a strong Cable Network ad revenue growing 26% YoY.

However, despite the increase in revenue (helped by one-off events such as the Super Bowl), the sharp drop in operating margin and EBITDA in the TV division may have signaled higher costs. Television EBITDA collapsed from $145 million to $60 million, as the cost of rights and production significantly expanded, leading to consolidated EBITDA margins of 19.6% versus 25.6% in the same period last year.

But my view is that without the high-cost Super Bowl rights dragging down TV EBITDA, margins in the TV segment should rebound somewhat in FQ4. I also see Cable Networks should stay stable with solid political ad tailwind as well as Tubi still dilutes margin a bit (low margin vs linear TV), but it's still a net positive for top-line.

So I see the downward revisions in FOX's EPS as a reflection of a combination of compressed margins, but potentially FQ4 bringing strong cash flows, as pointed out by the management team, given seasonal working capital issues, with advertising receivables to be reversed from the second half of this year.

But all in all, I'd say the odds are more in FOX's favor to deliver an "ok" result in FQ4, followed by a seasonal "hangover", with weaker quarterly revenues (without the Super Bowl), but potentially more stable or slightly improving margins and profits, supported by Tubi's growth and Cable's stability.

Risks and Final Remarks

It's not that there aren't structural risks for Fox Corp. (audience migration to streamign, cost of sports rights only growing, and Tubi still needing to prove significant profits), but I see this scenario as already known, and not a new factor that overturns the thesis in FQ4.

But naturally, as greater volatility in terms of operating profit pressured by such secular industry headwinds may come to punish FOX Corp. eventually, its defensive moat that protects consistent returns on invested capital tends to be the main point to monitor.

That said, I would summarize the Fox Corp. thesis as a defensive bet on a traditional media business that, despite secular challenges, still manages to generate economic value above its cost of capital, convert much of its operating profit into free cash flow, and return that cash to shareholders in a disciplined manner.

With an ROIC consistently above WACC, an attractive earnings yield relative to risk, and premium brands that are difficult to replicate—Fox News and the live sports rights—FOX behaves like a resilient cash cow in a mature sector.

data.title: Mastercard Q2 Preview: All Eyes On Stablecoins
data.description:
Between traditional and digital

Mastercard ($MA) is no longer a credit card network, but a strategic global infrastructure, increasingly close to becoming a hybrid platform between traditional and digital finance. There is still some appreciation for the stock, which the market considers “boring,” premium but predictable, good for riding the wave of consumer growth and little else. My thesis is different: I believe that Mastercard is building, beneath the surface, a set of still undervalued options that could radically change the stock's trajectory in the coming years. From the penetration of stablecoins (already regulated in Europe with USDG) to AI-based integration in the fight against fraud, to expansion into real-time payments, Mastercard is planting the right seeds at a time when the narrative on Big Tech is beginning to revolve around infrastructure and no longer just content.

It is certainly not a stock that will double in value. But it is one of the few cases where fundamental quality (margins >58%, FCF conversion above 50%, clear pricing power) is accompanied by evolving strategic leverage. And for a medium-term investor, it is precisely this mix (visibility + optionality) that represents an interesting combination.

One network, two souls

Those who view Mastercard from afar tend to simplify it: it is a card circuit, period. But the reality is much more interesting than one might think. Today, 40% of revenues come from value-added solutions that go far beyond swiping a card. We are talking about cybersecurity, anti-fraud AI, digital identity solutions, and open banking. And this second soul (less visible, but increasingly relevant) is what I believe will drive the revaluation of the stock in the coming years.

The core business remains very solid: the Payment Network segment, based on GDV volumes and switching fees, generates over 60% of revenues and maintains textbook operating margins (58.5% compared to an industry average of 34%). The acceptance network is vast: 150 million physical locations and 250 million digital touchpoints. But it is the technological infrastructure that is evolving rapidly. Following the acquisition of Recorded Future for $2.7 billion, Mastercard has enhanced its real-time threat intelligence capabilities, integrating them into risk management and AI-based services offered to partner banks.

Geographically, diversification is also robust: approximately 45% of revenues come from North America, with the rest balanced between EMEA and Asia-Pacific, with above-average growth in India and Southeast Asia. It is one of the few names in the sector that can grow both in developed markets (thanks to the upsell of digital services) and in emerging markets (thanks to financial inclusion and physical expansion).

Q2 2025 Earnings Preview: Strength Lies in the Details

For the second quarter of 2025, the Bloomberg consensus expects adjusted EPS of $4.02 and revenues of $7.94 billion, with an operating margin of around 59.3%. These are solid numbers, confirming the double-digit growth trajectory (YoY revenue +9.5%, cross-border +15.5%), but they do not tell the whole story.

Mastercard is not in the spotlight today because the market almost takes it for granted that it will “do its job.” The real issue is not beating the consensus but reigniting the narrative about what Mastercard is becoming, namely a high-margin infrastructure operator that combines payments, AI, digital identity, and stablecoins.

Personally, I won't be impressed by a $0.05 beat on EPS. I will focus on:

  • the dynamics of revenue from value-added services, especially those related to security, real-time payments, and identity (Recorded Future);
  • any comments on the expansion of the USDG project in the US, following its European launch with Robinhood and Kraken;
  • the evolution of client incentives, which are expected to reach $4.9 billion: an increase in line with the strategy of penetration among large merchants.

Furthermore, the quality of the beat (if it comes) will matter more than the beat itself. If volumes rise but take-rates come under pressure, or if margins are squeezed due to expansion costs in stablecoins and AI, the market could react coldly. But if the company proves it can sustain growth while maintaining control of operating leverage, Mastercard could be revalued more as an “AI-enabled fintech infrastructure stock” than as a defensive stock in payments.

That's why Mastercard could be worth $650

When I look at Mastercard, I don't just see a payments company: I see a margin machine, with a business model that prints cash in every market cycle. Unlike many of the more talked-about growth stocks, we're talking about a company with expected ROE above 200% as early as 2025 and an EBITDA margin exceeding 60%. At these levels of efficiency, the multiple is not a risk: it's a consequence.

Based on Bloomberg estimates, I have built a valuation model starting from an expected EBITDA of $19.646 billion for 2025.

Considering Mastercard's characteristics (duopolistic leadership, expansion of cross-border volumes, and pricing power on value-added services) I applied an EV/EBITDA multiple of 32x, consistent with the high end of historical valuations.

So:

  • Enterprise value (EV): $19.646 billion × 32 = $628.672 billion
  • Expected net debt: approximately $9 billion, therefore equity value = $619.672 billion
  • Diluted shares outstanding: approximately 951 million
  • Fair value per share = approximately $650

This assessment does not claim to be perfect, but it reflects a very simple idea. In my opinion, Mastercard deserves a premium because it is turning every evolution in payments into a competitive advantage. And if cash flows remain this strong, the market will return to paying it what it is worth.

Personal note: Personally, I still see the 32x multiple as justified. The new wave of AI-driven solutions, integration with regulated stablecoins, and increased revenues from value-added services are not yet fully reflected in the numbers. And Mastercard (as it often does) could surprise on the upside in the next two quarters.

Stablecoins, antitrust, and new challenges

It's true, Mastercard seems like a fortress today. But even fortresses need to update their defenses. The stablecoin market – considered a marginal issue for years – has suddenly become a strategic front, and Mastercard is neck-deep in it. The news that Amazon and Walmart are evaluating proprietary payment solutions based on stablecoins radically changes the competitive landscape. It is no longer a war between networks, but between models.

Mastercard has responded by forging alliances with players such as Robinhood, Fiserv, and Paxos to facilitate the adoption of regulated stablecoins (USDG, FIUSD) in compliance with the new European MiCA regulatory framework. But the truth is that if these initiatives take hold, Mastercard risks seeing part of its volume slip outside its perimeter, bypassing the traditional circuit altogether. At least, that is the fear hanging over the market.

And then there is the antitrust issue, which recurs cyclically. The recent legal victory in the US against a lawsuit alleging collusion with Apple was a positive sign, but the litigation in the UK (with over 500 retailers involved in the interchange fees case) remains an open wound. It is difficult to say whether all this will lead to sanctions, fee revisions, or simply background noise, but we cannot ignore that part of the long-term narrative, that of the efficiency of the duopoly, is now under scrutiny.

Personally, I believe that anyone investing in Mastercard today must accept the idea that its competitive advantage is less secure than it seems. But that does not make it any less profitable. If the company manages to transform the stablecoin risk into a new interoperable infrastructure, then we are not talking about a threat, but about evolution. And Mastercard has already proven, historically, that it knows how to evolve better than anyone else.

The real strength of Mastercard

Many focus solely on GDV (gross dollar volume) or transaction fees. But the reality is that Mastercard has built a second growth engine in recent years, one that is much less visible but much more stable: value-added services. Today, these represent almost 40% of total revenue and continue to expand faster than the traditional business.

We are talking about cybersecurity, tokenization, identity verification, open banking, real-time payment solutions, and even analytics for banks and merchants. These are all high-margin, non-cyclical areas that strengthen customer relationships. If the world stopped using physical cards tomorrow, Mastercard would still have a pipeline of organically growing services.

In my opinion, this is where the stock's rerating potential lies: no longer just as a “network,” but as a full-stack payment infrastructure. The recent acquisition of Recorded Future for $2.7 billion is a step in this direction: it integrates advanced threat intelligence into payment flows, bringing Mastercard to the heart of real-time decision automation.

Risks and opportunities

No brand is immune. Not even Mastercard. And anyone who ignores the risks that this business model entails has failed to understand where we are headed. The first and most obvious risk is regulatory. From British courts to the European Commission to the US Congress, large payment networks are increasingly under scrutiny. The recent fine imposed by the Bank of England on Vocalink is the latest example. Not so much for the amount ($16 million), but for the signal it sends: regulators are now also monitoring infrastructure operations, not just fees.

At the macro level, the second risk is the compression of transaction volumes in a scenario of global economic slowdown. Mastercard is more exposed than Visa to cross-border transactions, and this worked very well during the post-COVID reopening. But if global growth slows down (and the IMF has already revised its estimates downward), it will be difficult to maintain the same pace. Added to this is a technological risk that is often mentioned but rarely fully understood: disintermediation via stablecoins.

Amazon and Walmart are exploring the creation of their own digital currencies. The same goes for Robinhood with USDG, backed by Mastercard, Kraken, and Paxos. And the US Congress has already passed a framework law (the GENIUS Act). In other words, the future is no longer “if,” but “when.”

Personally, I don't believe that stablecoins will destroy the Mastercard model, quite the contrary. BUT companies that fail to integrate them into their stack risk being marginalized. Mastercard is trying to move ahead of the curve: it has entered into partnerships with Fiserv, Chainlink, Paxos, and ZeroHash to test interoperability between fiat and stablecoins. This is a smart attempt to integrate the threat before it becomes systemic.

Not everything is at risk. There are also underestimated opportunities. The first? Strengthening its position in emerging markets, where digital payment penetration is still low but growth is explosive. In India, in particular, Mastercard is forging strategic partnerships, aware that the future of cashless payments lies with the next 3 billion digital users.

The second opportunity concerns high-margin services, often ignored by retail investors. As mentioned above, these currently account for 40% of revenues, but could exceed 50% within 2-3 years. And finally, the real “gem”: Mastercard is quietly becoming a data infrastructure, with expansion into the segments of fraud detection, alternative scoring, and cybersecurity that could transform it into a strategic asset for governments and central banks as well. A bit like Palantir, but without the hype risk.

Why Mastercard deserves trust

The market has now realized that Mastercard is no longer a transaction processor. It is an infrastructure. It is a critical node in the global payment system. And, perhaps even more importantly, it is one of the few operators capable of adapting before the revolution arrives. It does not suffer from the issue of stablecoins: it studies it, experiments with it, and incorporates it. It does not ignore regulatory pressure: it faces it head-on, even at the cost of paying fines. Margins, even in this challenging environment, remain very high. And cash generation is so solid that it allows for constant buybacks and growing dividends without impacting financial leverage.

With estimated EBITDA for 2025 of $19.6 billion and an extremely solid net financial position, we have estimated a fair value of around $615 per share, implying a potential upside of +15% from current levels. In a more favorable scenario, with acceleration in value-added services, re-acceleration of cross-border transactions, and stable growth in e-commerce in emerging countries, the stock could even reach $650, a threshold already mentioned above as the implicit “upper bound” in several valuation models.

But the real gem? Mastercard is quietly building a global graph of digital identities and payment habits. Its assets in behavioral data, authentication solutions, and fraud prevention could become a new standard for governments and central banks grappling with digital identity over the next five years. This is not science fiction: it has already signed strategic partnerships with countries such as Singapore, the United Kingdom, and Canada on these fronts.

This is the part of the story that the market is perhaps still underestimating. And that we can take advantage of today.

  • Rating: Buy
  • Target: $615
  • Ideal weight in portfolio in a balanced: 3–5%, accumulate on pullbacks below $520 (there, strong buy)
